claim.content: In a two-dimensional electron Fermi liquid realized in GaAs/AlGaAs quantum wells, the density-of-states effective mass m_{e}^{\mathrm{FL}} defined by m_{e}^{\mathrm{FL}}=\dfrac{\pi\hbar^{2} n_{S}}{E_{F}} (where n_{S} is the two-dimensional electron concentration, E_{F} is the spectroscopic Fermi energy measured from the size-quantization band bottom, \hbar is Planck’s constant divided by 2\pi, and all quantities refer to a single occupied subband) increases as the electron density decreases; equivalently, m_{e}^{\mathrm{FL}} increases with the interaction parameter r_{S}=1/\bigl[a_{B}(\pi n_{S})^{1/2}\bigr] (where a_{B} is the Bohr radius). Quantitatively, when r_{S} is increased from about 1 to about 4.5 the measured m_{e}^{\mathrm{FL}} increases by approximately 35% relative to the band cyclotron mass in GaAs (the standard GaAs band cyclotron mass used for comparison is m_{e}=0.067\,m_{0}, where m_{0} is the free-electron mass).
